summary: American Southern Gothic author known for The Heart Is a Lonely Hunter, The Member of the Wedding, The Ballad of the Sad Cafe, and Reflections in a Golden Eye. Her works frequently feature tomboyish young women, deaf-mute characters, and themes of loneliness and isolation in the American South.

works.name: The Heart Is a Lonely Hunter
works.description: McCullers's debut and best-known novel, originally titled The Mute, about the deaf-mute John Singer who works as a jewelry repairman. Singer presents a card to those he meets explaining how to communicate with him. Four characters confide in Singer: Dr. Benedict Mady Copeland (a Black physician dying of tuberculosis who named his son Karl Marx — called "Buddy" — and delivers a Christmas speech about Karl Marx, giving a five-dollar award to Lancy Davis for his essay on organizing a Black revolt); Jake Blount (an alcoholic who beats his head against bricks and transitions from evangelist to advocate for workers' rights); Mick Kelly (a young tomboy who hears the Eroica Symphony on a rich neighbor's radio hiding in bushes, tries to build a homemade violin, quits school to work at Woolworth's, has sex with Harry Minowitz, and scares her brother Bubber into running away after he accidentally shot Baby Wilson with a BB gun — Bubber is told he'll be executed in the electric chair); and Biff Brannon (who runs the New York Cafe). Singer's best friend Spiros Antonapoulos becomes a kleptomaniac and urinates in public; he is committed to a mental asylum and dies there; Singer shoots himself in the chest upon learning of his death. Richard Wright praised McCullers for handling Negro characters 'with as much ease and justice as those of her own race.' Azar Nafisi discusses this novel in The Republic of Imagination.

works.name: The Member of the Wedding
works.description: A novel about the tomboyish twelve-year-old Frankie Addams who obsesses over the wedding of her brother Jarvis and his bride Janice, dreaming of joining them on their honeymoon to Alaska. She renames herself "F. Jasmine" and wants to become part of "the we of me." The novel features the housekeeper Berenice Sadie Brown, and Frankie's cousin John Henry West. The novel opens with Frankie playing cards with John Henry and Berenice. A drunken red-haired soldier makes a date with Frankie; later she crawls down a fire escape from the hotel room of a soldier who forces himself on her after buying her a drink at the Blue Moon Cafe, and she hits a soldier over the head with a pitcher during a sexual assault. Frankie fantasizes about donating so much blood to the Red Cross that veterans come to thank her personally. McCullers adapted the novel into a successful Broadway play. Composed while at February House.

works.name: The Ballad of the Sad Cafe
works.description: A novella about Miss Amelia Evans, who loses a fight with her former husband of ten days, Marvin Macy, after the intervention of the hunchbacked Cousin Lymon. Marvin Macy (who carries the ear of a man he killed in a razor fight) returns from prison and ransacks the cafe with Cousin Lymon, who wriggles his ears and tugs on Marvin Macy's pants leg for attention. The story examines themes of unrequited love: the narrator observes "most of us would rather love than be loved." A character is called a "Morris Finestein" for crying. The rumors of a murder are started by a character named Merlie Ryan who suffers from three-day malaria.

works.name: Reflections in a Golden Eye
works.description: A novel set on a military base about the homosexual Captain Weldon Penderton, whose wife Leonora has an affair with Major Langdon. Private Ellgee Williams is attracted to Leonora and spies on her after clearing tree branches. The novel features the Filipino houseboy Anacleto, who helps Alison Langdon around the house after she cuts off her nipples. Tennessee Williams wrote the introduction defending its portrayal of homosexuality. Made into a film with Elizabeth Taylor and Marlon Brando.

works.name: General / Biographical
works.description: McCullers was a Southern Gothic author from Columbus, Georgia (whose segregated university rejected her archive). She co-founded the queer literary commune February House with W.H. Auden, where they lived with Benjamin Britten, Klaus Mann, and Gypsy Rose Lee. Her close friend Tennessee Williams wrote the introduction to Reflections in a Golden Eye. Suzanne Vega explored her relationship with photographer Annemarie Schwarzenbach in an album. Jenn Shapland wrote about her "retroactive closeting by peers and biographers" in My Autobiography of Carson McCullers. Carlos Dews edited her memoir Illumination and Night Glare (unfinished) and is working on a book of her letters. Short story "A Tree. A Rock. A Cloud." describes a "science of love" through inanimate objects. Her failed play depicts a woman's affair with an architect while her husband is at a sanitarium.

clues.clue: Co-founded February House with W.H. Auden; lived with Britten, Klaus Mann, Gypsy Rose Lee

clues.clue: Short story "A Tree. A Rock. A Cloud." teaches a "science of love" starting with loving inanimate objects

comprehensive_summary:
Carson McCullers (1917-1967) is a Southern Gothic author from Columbus, Georgia whose works frequently feature tomboyish young women, deaf-mute characters, and themes of loneliness and unrequited love. Her debut novel The Heart Is a Lonely Hunter (originally titled The Mute) centers on deaf-mute jewelry repairman John Singer, who presents a card to explain how to communicate. Four characters confide in Singer: Dr. Benedict Mady Copeland (a Black physician with tuberculosis who named his son Karl Marx and delivers a Christmas speech on Marx; gives Lancy Davis a five-dollar award for an essay on organizing Black revolt); the alcoholic Jake Blount (who beats his head against bricks, transitioning from evangelist to labor advocate); the tomboy Mick Kelly (who hears the Eroica Symphony on a rich neighbor's radio, tries to build a homemade violin, quits school to work at Woolworth's, and scares her brother Bubber after he accidentally shot Baby Wilson with a BB gun); and cafe owner Biff Brannon. Singer's best friend Spiros Antonapoulos becomes a kleptomaniac and is committed to an asylum; Singer shoots himself upon hearing of his death. Richard Wright praised McCullers for handling Negro characters "with as much ease and justice as those of her own race." Azar Nafisi discusses the novel in The Republic of Imagination.

The Member of the Wedding follows twelve-year-old Frankie Addams (who renames herself "F. Jasmine") obsessing over her brother Jarvis's wedding to Janice, dreaming of joining their honeymoon to Alaska as part of "the we of me." The novel opens with Frankie playing cards with her cousin John Henry West and housekeeper Berenice Sadie Brown. Frankie later crawls down a fire escape from a soldier's hotel room at the Blue Moon Cafe. McCullers adapted the novel into a successful Broadway play. It was composed at February House.

The Ballad of the Sad Cafe centers on Miss Amelia Evans, who is defeated by her former ten-day husband Marvin Macy (who carries the ear of a man killed in a razor fight) with the help of Cousin Lymon, the hunchback who wiggles his ears and tugs on Macy's pants for attention. The narrator observes "most of us would rather love than be loved."

Reflections in a Golden Eye, set on a military base, features the homosexual Captain Penderton, his wife Leonora's affair with Major Langdon, Private Williams's obsessive spying on Leonora, and the Filipino houseboy Anacleto helping Alison Langdon (who has cut off her nipples). Tennessee Williams wrote its introduction defending the portrayal of homosexuality. It was made into a film with Elizabeth Taylor and Marlon Brando.

McCullers co-founded the queer literary commune February House with W.H. Auden (where Britten, Klaus Mann, and Gypsy Rose Lee also lived). Her short story "A Tree. A Rock. A Cloud." outlines a character's "science of love" starting with inanimate objects. Her unfinished autobiography is Illumination and Night Glare.
